periods.title: The pre-reclamation island
periods.summary: The 1859 U.S. Coast Survey chart is the earliest detailed island-wide reference currently assigned to the project. It predates Trumbo Point, the Overseas Railway, and the major twentieth-century reclamation projects.
highlights: Use the Coast Survey shoreline as the principal early-island reference once it is georeferenced and reviewed.
The chart shows the western Florida Keys and Key West before the railroad terminal transformed the north shore.
The archival image is public domain, but it is not yet published as traced shoreline geometry.

archivalMap.title: 1859 U.S. Coast Survey chart
archivalMap.caption: Public-domain Coast Survey chart. Open the source for the full-resolution sheet.

periods.title: Western Key West before Trumbo
periods.summary: NOAA’s GC20A10 data-rescue project records a 1904 shoreline survey of Key West’s western end. Its documented coverage is displayed on the map; the actual shoreline vector still must be exported from NOAA’s Shoreline Data Explorer.
highlights: The source is dated January 1, 1904 and covers the western end of Key West.
The displayed rectangle is the NOAA project coverage envelope—not the historical shoreline.
This survey provides a high-value pre-terminal comparison for the western waterfront.

rasterOverlay.title: 1907 Admiralty harbor chart
rasterOverlay.attribution: 1907 Admiralty chart via Wikimaps Warper; 5 control points

periods.title: Harbor immediately before the terminal fill
periods.summary: A 1907 Admiralty chart has been georeferenced in Wikimaps Warper and can be overlaid directly on the modern map. It is the first working historical image layer in this project.
highlights: Drag the chart-opacity control to compare the 1907 shoreline and harbor with the modern basemap.
The chart is useful for broad interpretation but has only five published control points, so it is not treated as survey-grade geometry.
Trumbo’s large terminal landfill does not yet dominate the northern waterfront.

rasterOverlay.title: 1907 Admiralty harbor chart
rasterOverlay.attribution: 1907 Admiralty chart via Wikimaps Warper; 5 control points

periods.title: Trumbo Point transforms the north shore
periods.summary: NOAA’s GC20A04 historical shoreline project is dated 1912 and covers the Key West waterfront, including the Trumbo study area. The first Florida East Coast Railway train reached Key West on January 22, 1912.
highlights: KWAHS describes a 134-acre landfill inspected in 1911; NAVFAC describes a 140-acre landfill area created in 1908. The project preserves both figures until the underlying acreage definitions can be reconciled.
KWAHS records the first spike at the Trumbo railhead in May 1910 and the first train’s arrival in January 1912.
The NOAA survey coverage envelope is visible; the actual 1912 shoreline vector remains the next major data import.

rasterOverlay.title: 1907 pre-terminal comparison chart
rasterOverlay.attribution: 1907 Admiralty chart via Wikimaps Warper; 5 control points

periods.title: The railway era closes
periods.summary: The Labor Day hurricane destroyed large portions of the Overseas Extension in September 1935. The financially distressed Florida East Coast Railway did not rebuild the route to Key West.
highlights: The terminal landfill remained even after direct railway service ended.
Rail infrastructure and terminal functions must be separated from the permanent land reclamation beneath them.
Aerial or mapped evidence is still needed to publish a defensible 1935 terminal footprint.

periods.title: Trumbo in the postwar harbor
periods.summary: NOAA’s PH5805 data-rescue project is dated December 12, 1957 and covers Key West Harbor. Its source record documents vectorized historical shoreline and mapped land, water, and man-made features.
highlights: The project coverage includes Trumbo and adjacent harbor waters.
This is the strongest identified mid-century NOAA shoreline source for comparing the rail-era terminal with postwar naval development.
The displayed rectangle is survey-project coverage, not an inferred shoreline.

periods.title: Housing, aviation, and military growth
periods.summary: The mid-1960s layer remains an aerial-photography reconstruction target. It will capture changes around Trumbo, New Town, the airport, Sigsbee, and Fleming Key without pretending that one image date represents the entire island.
highlights: NAVFAC records that part of Trumbo was developed for housing during the 1960s.
Fleming Key had already been created as a dredge-spoil island in the early 1940s.
Dated University of Florida aerial frames are required before publishing an island-wide 1960s boundary.

periods.title: Modern Key West shoreline
periods.summary: NOAA’s FL1611-TB-N project used lidar and imagery collected April 14–25, 2016 to compile a high-resolution Key West shoreline. The map also requests NOAA’s live CUSP shoreline service as a modern comparison line.
highlights: The 2016 NOAA project extent is displayed as metadata coverage.
The live blue shoreline comes from NOAA’s CUSP ArcGIS service when that service is reachable.
CUSP is a mean-high-water proxy product with lighter quality-control requirements than NOAA’s National Shoreline and omits some alongshore features such as piers.
